skill.name: attack-tree-construction
skill.description: Build comprehensive attack trees to visualize threat paths. Use when mapping attack scenarios, identifying defense gaps, or communicating security risks to stakeholders.
skill.summary: Build comprehensive attack trees to visualize threat paths. Use when mapping attack scenarios, ident...
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, weak cryptography, malware keywords, reconnaissance terms, and hardcoded URLs. AI review found these are Markdown code fences, benign Python modeling examples, threat-model vocabulary, and documentation links. No prompt injection, exfiltration, runtime network access, or executable malicious behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positive: Markdown Code Fences
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown code block delimiters and Python examples. They are not shell backticks, subprocess calls, or executable commands run by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are fenced documentation examples and diagram text. No command execution API, shell invocation, or user-controlled execution path is present.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positive: Threat Modeling Vocabulary
low_findings.description: The keylogger, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance findings are security-analysis terms used inside an attack-tree model. They describe attack scenarios and mitigations, not malware behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The terms appear in a defensive attack-tree construction guide. The file does not provide malware implementation, credential theft logic, or reconnaissance commands.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positive: Reference URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are resource links to Schneier, MITRE ATT&CK, and OWASP. They are not used by executable code and do not transmit local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plain Markdown bibliography links. There is no network client, request construction, or data exfiltration path tied to them.

content.user_title: Map Attack Paths With Attack Trees
content.value_statement: Security teams need a clear way to explain how attackers can reach sensitive goals. This skill builds structured attack trees with paths, attributes, mitigations, and export formats.
actual_capabilities: Defines attack tree nodes with OR, AND, and leaf semantics.
Models attack difficulty, cost, time, detection risk, and mitigation coverage.
Provides Python templates for building, exporting, and analyzing trees.
Exports attack trees to JSON, Mermaid, and PlantUML style formats.
Identifies easiest, cheapest, stealthiest, and least mitigated attack paths.
Prioritizes mitigations by path coverage impact.
limitations: It does not validate whether modeled attacks are currently exploitable.
It depends on accurate inputs from security experts and system owners.
It does not run scanners, exploit systems, or collect live telemetry.
Large systems may need decomposition into several smaller trees.

use_cases.title: Plan Defensive Investments
use_cases.description: Map likely attack paths and compare which mitigations block the most routes to a critical asset.

use_cases.title: Prepare Penetration Test Scope
use_cases.description: Turn business goals and system boundaries into structured paths that guide legal and controlled testing.

use_cases.title: Explain Risk to Stakeholders
use_cases.description: Create readable diagrams that show why specific controls reduce risk across multiple attack scenarios.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Attack Tree
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner threat modeling for one asset
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an attack tree for protecting [asset]. Use OR, AND, and leaf nodes. Include likely mitigations for each leaf attack.

prompt_templates.title: Add Path Attributes
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an attack tree for [scenario]. Add difficulty, cost, time, detection risk, and required mitigations for every leaf node.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Mitigation Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this attack tree and identify open paths, critical nodes, and mitigations with the highest coverage impact: [tree details].

prompt_templates.title: Generate Executive Diagram Outputs
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this attack tree into a Mermaid diagram and an executive summary. Highlight easiest paths, stealthiest paths, and priority defenses.

output_examples.input: Attack tree for unauthorized access to a customer account.
output_examples.output: A structured tree with credential theft, authentication bypass, and social engineering branches, each mapped to mitigations like MFA and rate limiting.

output_examples.input: Coverage analysis for proposed controls on an account takeover tree.
output_examples.output: A prioritized mitigation list showing which controls block the most paths, plus remaining open paths that need review.

output_examples.input: Diagram request for a payment system attack model.
output_examples.output: A Mermaid-ready attack tree with clear OR and AND branches, color-coded leaf difficulty, and concise stakeholder notes.

best_practices: Define the attacker goal and protected asset before creating branches.
Include mitigations and detection assumptions for every leaf attack.
Review the tree with system owners, defenders, and authorized testers.
anti_patterns: Do not use the tree as proof that a system is exploitable.
Do not mix unrelated assets into one large unclear tree.
Do not omit assumptions about access, scope, and authorization.

faq.question: Does this skill perform attacks?
faq.answer: No. It helps model attack paths for defensive planning and authorized security analysis.

faq.question: Can it export diagrams?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes templates for Mermaid and PlantUML style attack tree outputs.

faq.question: Who should use it?
faq.answer: Security architects, penetration test planners, risk teams, and security leaders can use it for structured threat discussions.

faq.question: Does it replace expert review?
faq.answer: No. Attack trees should be validated by people who understand the system and threat environment.

faq.question: Can it prioritize mitigations?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes analysis patterns for ranking mitigations by attack path coverage impact.

faq.question: Is it compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
